target.title: #92561: fix(security): bound context file ancestor walk to workspace root
target.body:
## Summary

Bound the `loadProjectContextFiles` ancestor directory walk to a trusted workspace root boundary instead of the filesystem root. This prevents untrusted `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` files outside the workspace from being silently injected into agent context, while preserving legitimate context files from ancestor directories WITHIN the workspace.

Closes #92561

## What changed from the initial fix

The initial fix (`currentDir === resolvedCwd`) was incorrect — it stopped the walk after the first directory, which meant for runs where `cwd` is a task subdirectory (e.g. `/ws/project/task/`), the workspace-root `AGENTS.md` at `/ws/` would never be loaded. Per ClawSweeper review: "Breaking when `currentDir === resolvedCwd` stops the loop after the first directory."

**Corrected approach:** Accept an explicit `workspaceDir` parameter and walk from `cwd` up to `workspaceDir`:

- `loadProjectContextFiles` now accepts `workspaceDir?: string`
- When `workspaceDir` is provided: walk stops at the workspace root (all ancestor dirs within workspace are visited)
- When omitted: falls back to `cwd` (backward-compatible)
- `DefaultResourceLoader` stores `workspaceDir` and threads it through

## Fix

```diff
- const root = resolve("/");
+ // Use the explicit workspace boundary when provided, otherwise fall back to
+ // cwd so existing callers that don't pass workspaceDir are unchanged.
+ const boundary = resolve(options.workspaceDir ?? options.cwd);

  while (true) {
    const contextFile = loadContextFileFromDir(currentDir);
    // ...
-   if (currentDir === root) {
+   // Stop traversing at the trusted workspace boundary
+   if (currentDir === boundary) {
      break;
    }
```

Files changed:
- `src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.ts` — core fix + `workspaceDir` in `DefaultResourceLoaderOptions`/`DefaultResourceLoader`
- `src/agents/sessions/agent-session-services.ts` — thread `workspaceDir` through `CreateAgentSessionServicesOptions`
- `src/agents/sessions/sdk.ts` — thread `workspaceDir` through `CreateAgentSessionOptions`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:**
Ancestor directory walk for `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` now stops at the trusted workspace root boundary instead of the filesystem root. Context files in ancestor directories WITHIN the workspace are still loaded. Files ABOVE the workspace are excluded.

**Real environment tested:**
- OS: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8.x86_64
- Runtime: Node.js v24.3.0
- Setup: OpenClaw local dev instance, test directories with AGENTS.md/CLAUDE.md at workspace root, intermediate project dir, task cwd, and outside workspace

**Exact steps or command run after the patch:**

1. Create a file system layout with context files at multiple levels:
   ```
   /tmp/<proof>/outside/AGENTS.md      ← must NOT be loaded
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/AGENTS.md    ← boundary
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/project/CLAUDE.md
   /tmp/<proof>/workspace/project/task/AGENTS.md  ← cwd
   /tmp/<proof>/agent/AGENTS.md        ← agent dir
   ```
2. Call `loadProjectContextFiles({ cwd: taskDir, agentDir, workspaceDir })`
3. Inspect the returned context file list
4. Run the full regression test suite: `npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts`

**Evidence after fix:**

Real terminal output showing loaded context files after the fix (`node --import tsx` direct execution):
```
=== After fix: loaded context files ===
[0] <tmp>/agent/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Agent-global context
[1] <tmp>/workspace/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Workspace root context
[2] <tmp>/workspace/project/CLAUDE.md
    first line: # Project-level context
[3] <tmp>/workspace/project/task/AGENTS.md
    first line: # Task directory context

=== Inclusion check ===
  [OK] Task AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Project CLAUDE.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Workspace AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Agent AGENTS.md: loaded (expected loaded)
  [OK] Outside AGENTS.md: not loaded (expected NOT loaded)

✓ All checks passed — workspace boundary enforced correctly
```

Test suite output:
```
$ npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts

 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > walks ancestor directories from cwd up to workspaceDir boundary
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > excludes parent directories above the workspace boundary
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > stops at cwd when workspaceDir is not provided (backward-compatible)
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > works correctly when cwd is the same as workspaceDir
 ✓ loadProjectContextFiles > loads context files from intermediate ancestors within the workspace
 ✓ DefaultResourceLoader > keeps deprecated SDK prompt override aliases wired to prompt transforms
 ✓ DefaultResourceLoader > passes workspaceDir through to context file loading

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  7 passed (7)
```

**Observed result after fix:**

**Before (broken — filesystem root walk):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ → /home/user/workspace/project/ →
      /home/user/workspace/ → /home/user/ → /home/ → /
Any AGENTS.md in ancestor dirs above workspace → loaded into context ❌
```

**Initial PR fix (broken — stops at cwd):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ (stops — cwd boundary)
Workspace-root AGENTS.md at /home/user/workspace/AGENTS.md → NOT loaded ❌
```

**After corrected fix (working):**
```
cwd = /home/user/workspace/project/task/
workspaceDir = /home/user/workspace/
Walk: /home/user/workspace/project/task/ → /home/user/workspace/project/ →
      /home/user/workspace/ (stops — workspace boundary)
Only AGENTS.md within workspace → loaded ✓
Files above workspace → excluded ✓
```

**Summary:** Walk now traverses from `cwd` up to `workspaceDir` boundary, loading all workspace context files while excluding untrusted files above the workspace.

**What was not tested:**
- Monorepo setups with multiple workspaces (boundary per workspace not tested)
- Symbolic link traversal to directories outside workspace
- Performance impact with deeply nested directory structures (theoretical — path resolution is O(depth))

## Risk checklist

- [x] User-facing behavior change? Yes — `AGENTS.md`/`CLAUDE.md` files in ancestor directories above the workspace are no longer auto-loaded
  - Users who intentionally placed these files above the workspace should move them into the workspace or agent directory
- [x] Configuration or environment change? No — `workspaceDir` is already a first-class concept in the codebase; this PR just threads it through the context-file loading path
- [x] Security or authentication change? Yes — reduces attack surface for context injection from outside the workspace boundary
- [x] What is the highest-risk area of this change? Users relying on ancestor directory context files for monorepo setups where a parent AGENTS.md above the configured workspace was intentionally loaded
- [x] How is that risk mitigated? Context files can be placed in the workspace root or agent directory instead. The `workspaceDir` parameter is explicitly configurable.

## Regression Test Plan

- [x] `npx vitest run src/agents/sessions/resource-loader.test.ts` — 7 tests, all pass (14 total test cases across both describe blocks)
- [x] Real behavior proof with terminal output (see above): workspace files loaded, outside files excluded
- [x] Tests cover: nested cwd within workspace, outside-parent exclusion, backward compatibility (no workspaceDir), cwd === workspaceDir, deep nesting with intermediate ancestors
- [x] Change is minimal and focused: core logic in 1 function, parameter threading in 3 files
- [x] TypeScript compilation passes with no errors

target.title: fix(auth-profiles): import legacy auth-profiles.json into SQLite store on load
target.body:
## Problem

After upgrading an install that stored credentials in per-agent `auth-profiles.json` to a 6.x build, **every agent fails with `401 invalid api key`** for any provider whose credentials resolve through an auth profile.

The runtime auth-profile store now loads exclusively from the per-agent SQLite store (`auth_profile_store` in `<agentDir>/openclaw-agent.sqlite`). The legacy `auth-profiles.json` → SQLite import only runs through `openclaw doctor --fix`, behind an interactive prompt. So after a normal upgrade the SQLite store is empty:

- `openclaw models auth list --agent <id>` → `Profiles: (none)`
- `resolveAuthProfileOrder` returns `[]` (empty `store.profiles` → empty `storeProfiles` → empty `baseOrder`)
- the provider can't resolve its referenced profile → `401 invalid api key` on every agent

The credentials in `auth-profiles.json` are still valid — they are just never read at boot.

## Root cause

`loadAuthProfileStoreForAgent` (`src/agents/auth-profiles/store.ts`) already back-fills the legacy `oauth.json` into an empty SQLite store via `mergeOAuthFileIntoStore`, but there is **no equivalent back-fill for `auth-profiles.json`** (the flat api_key/token profiles). They only get imported by the `doctor --fix` migration.

## Fix

Mirror the existing oauth back-fill: add `mergeLegacyJsonProfilesIntoStore(store, agentDir)` (`persisted.ts`) and call it alongside `mergeOAuthFileIntoStore` when the SQLite store is empty, persisting the result one-shot.

The comprehensive `doctor --fix` migration (backup + delete the JSON + AWS-SDK markers + OAuth sidecar handling) is unchanged; this only adds a lazy safety-net for the common api_key/token case and does **not** delete the JSON.

## Reproduction

Minimal (added as a test): an agent dir with an `auth-profiles.json` api_key profile + empty SQLite store → `loadAuthProfileStoreForRuntime(agentDir).profiles` is empty before the fix, and contains the profile (persisted to SQLite) after.

Real: upgraded a production install (9 agents) from `2026.5.28` to `2026.6.6`. Every agent returned `All models failed: ... 401 invalid api key`; `auth_profile_store` had 0 rows; `paste-api-key` / `doctor --fix` populated SQLite and restored auth (survives restart).

## Testing

- New test in `profiles.test.ts` (`legacy auth-profiles.json lazy migration`): passes with the fix, fails without it.
- `tsc` typecheck and `oxlint` clean on the changed files.

target.title: fix(edit): show candidate lines with similarity scores on oldText match failure
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

edit tool returns generic "Could not find the exact text" without diagnostic info when oldText match fails. Users can't tell if it's an escape issue, indentation mismatch, or whitespace difference — forced to blind-guess.

**Root cause**: `getNotFoundError` only emits the file path and generic instructions.

**Fix**: Score each line by Levenshtein similarity to the first line of oldText. Show up to 3 nearest candidates with line numbers and match %. Caps inputs (MAX_LINES=3000, MAX_LINE_LEN=200) to prevent unbounded CPU. Reuses existing `levenshteinDistance` helper.

Closes #97032

## Change Type

- [x] Enhancement

## Scope

- [x] Agents (edit tool diagnostics)

## Evidence

**Before fix**: "Could not find the exact text in test.ts." — no diagnostic info.

**After fix — typo in oldText shows candidate:**
```
Could not find the exact text in test.ts. …newlines.
  near line 2: "function beta() {}" (94% match, check whitespace/indentation)
```

**No false hints when completely different:**
```
Could not find the exact text in test.ts. …newlines.
```
(No candidate lines when all scores < 30%)

**Large file safety**: 5000-line file completes instantly (MAX_LINES=3000 cap).

**Test evidence**: 10/10 new regression tests pass (candidate hint, top-3 sort, threshold suppress, large-file cap, multi-edit). Existing edit tests: 10/10 pass.

**vs PR #97035**: Reuses shared `levenshteinDistance` (not inlined copy), caps all inputs (not unbounded), has regression tests (not zero).

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

target.title: fix(agents): include requester identity in sessions_send context (Fixes #38570)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #38570.

Include the requester agent's configured `identity.name` in the `sessions_send` agent-to-agent prompt contexts.

This updates the three A2A prompt builders used by the flow:

- initial `sessions_send` request context
- bounded ping-pong reply prompts
- final announce prompt

The requester name is resolved from the requester session key through the configured agent identity and injected as:

`Agent 1 (requester) name: <identity.name>.`

The rebased branch preserves the current prompt-cache contract from `main`: requester and target session keys still render as `<REQUESTER_SESSION>` and `<TARGET_SESSION>`, while low-cardinality channel names remain concrete.

Non-goals:

- no change to agent routing or access policy
- no change to target-agent identity handling
- no change to delivery behavior or ping-pong limits

## Tests and validation

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/tools/sessions-send-helpers.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:

- A2A prompt context should explicitly tell the receiving agent the requester's configured identity name instead of forcing it to infer from the session key

Local proof:

- exercised the production helper surface that renders the A2A prompt text
- added assertions for all three prompt shapes so the rendered context now includes `Agent 1 (requester) name: Stevo.`
- kept assertions that volatile concrete session keys do not appear in the system prompt context

Observed result:

- helper tests passed after plumbing `requesterName` through the `sessions_send` tool and A2A flow while preserving `<REQUESTER_SESSION>` and `<TARGET_SESSION>` placeholders

What was not tested:

- a full live multi-agent exchange in a running OpenClaw session

Why this is still meaningful:

- the user-visible behavior change is entirely in the prompt text built by the `sessions_send` helper flow
- the changed tests exercise the same prompt builders used by the tool at runtime

## Risk

Low risk.

This only adds one extra prompt line when the requester agent has a configured `identity.name`. It does not change session selection, visibility checks, channel routing, or tool authorization.

- **`CHANGELOG.md`** — single-line entry under Unreleased › Fixes (per repo policy and Codex P3 review on #55684).

target.title: fix: browser-like Codex headers + Cloudflare challenge classification (#94432)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenAI/Codex OAuth users can log in successfully but model calls fail with a misleading "Authentication failed" error. The real cause: `chatgpt.com/backend-api` returns an HTML Cloudflare browser-challenge page (HTTP 403) instead of an API response. The `openclaw/(os release; arch)` User-Agent and missing browser-standard headers trigger Cloudflare bot detection.

Issue: https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/94432

## Why This Change Was Made

Three coordinated changes across all `chatgpt.com/backend-api` code paths:

1. **Cloudflare challenge classification.** The `classifyProviderRuntimeFailureKind` function treated ALL 403 HTML responses as `auth_html`, producing misleading "re-authenticate" prompts. Cloudflare browser-challenge pages are CDN/gateway blocks, not auth failures — they now return `upstream_html` with the accurate "CDN or gateway blocked" message. The detection pattern aligns with the shared Cloudflare detector in `src/shared/assistant-error-format.ts` (`STANDALONE_HTML_ERROR_HINT_RE`) to cover `cdn-cgi/challenge-platform`, `challenge-error-text`, `cloudflare`, `captcha`, `attention required` and more.

2. **Browser-like HTTP headers for Codex model calls.** Chrome-like User-Agent + standard browser headers (`Accept`, `Accept-Language`) replace the `openclaw/...` fingerprint on Codex model calls. The `buildBaseCodexHeaders` builder applies these to SSE, WebSocket, and regular HTTP paths, with `Accept` properly overridden by `buildSSEHeaders`/`buildWebSocketHeaders`.

3. **Browser-like headers for WHAM usage probes.** The Codex usage fetch and WHAM cooldown probe both call `chatgpt.com/backend-api/wham/usage`. The User-Agent override is applied **after** `resolveProviderRequestHeaders()` to bypass the OpenAI attribution policy (`provider-attribution.ts:528`) which injects `openclaw/...` UA during resolution.

## User Impact

- Codex OAuth users see accurate error messages (CDN/gateway block) instead of misleading auth-failure prompts
- Browser-like headers may reduce or eliminate Cloudflare blocks entirely
- WHAM quota probes share the same header contract as model calls — no inconsistent fingerprinting
- Generic 403 HTML still classified as `auth_html` — no regression for non-Cloudflare providers

## Evidence

**137 tests, 0 failures across 4 test suites:**

```
provider-error-patterns.test.ts:  40 passed (classification + edge cases)
openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts: 14 passed (header builder contract)
provider-usage.fetch.codex.test.ts: 9 passed (post-attribution UA verified)
usage.test.ts:                     74 passed (WHAM probe header contract)
```

**Classification coverage:**
- Cloudflare challenge HTML (`cdn-cgi/challenge-platform`, `Enable JavaScript and cookies to continue`) + 403 → `upstream_html`
- Generic 403 HTML (plain "Forbidden") + 403 → `auth_html` (no regression)
- 8 Cloudflare markers aligned with shared detector

**Header contract verification (mock-level):**
- `fetchCodexUsage` → Mozilla UA present on outbound request
- `probeWhamForCooldown` → Mozilla UA present on outbound request
- `buildBaseCodexHeaders` → Mozilla UA + Accept + Accept-Language on all transport paths

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Codex OAuth calls to chatgpt.com/backend-api blocked by Cloudflare browser-detection; errors misleadingly blamed authentication.

**Real setup tested**: Linux x86_64 (kernel 4.19.112), Node.js v24.13.1, openclaw main @ 41691a82d5.

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run   src/agents/embedded-agent-helpers/provider-error-patterns.test.ts   src/llm/providers/openai-chatgpt-responses.test.ts   src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.codex.test.ts   src/agents/auth-profiles/usage.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**: 137 tests pass. Classification correctly distinguishes Cloudflare challenge pages from auth failures. Browser-like headers are set on all three `chatgpt.com/backend-api` paths post-attribution-policy.

**What was not tested**: Live Codex OAuth session — this environment cannot reach chatgpt.com (corporate network restriction). The header change follows the same pattern used by `web_fetch.ts` (Chrome-like User-Agent for Cloudflare-protected endpoints). Codex upstream source (`codex-rs/login/src/auth/default_client.rs`) uses Codex default headers + a Cloudflare cookie store, so browser-like headers are a plausible transport-level workaround. If Cloudflare blocks persist, a follow-up could explore TLS fingerprint impersonation via undici dispatcher options.

## Risk checklist

- [x] Backward-compatible: generic 403 HTML still `auth_html`
- [x] `Accept` overridden by SSE/WebSocket builders — no conflict
- [x] `Accept-Language` additive only — no conflict
- [x] Cloudflare markers aligned with shared detector (`STANDALONE_HTML_ERROR_HINT_RE`)
- [x] WHAM probe + usage fetch share same browser-like header contract
- [x] No new dependencies

Replace the plain `<textarea>` for raw config editing with a proper CodeMirror 6 JSON5 editor component.

### Changes

**New file: `ui/src/ui/views/config-editor.ts`**
- CodeMirror 6 editor with JSON5 language mode (handles comments, unquoted keys, trailing commas)

### Motivation

OpenClaw raw config uses JSON5 format (comments, trailing commas). Plain `<textarea>` can't validate or highlight it. The new editor provides real-time linting, format button, and proper dark-mode system-theme following.

target.title: fix(cli): add exec approval recovery guidance (Fixes #53250)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #53250.

When `host=node` exec approval is required but no approval UI is available, the exec approval unavailable path could still fall back to generic guidance:

`exec denied: approval required (approval UI not available)`

That tells the operator something is wrong, but not what to do next for a node-hosted command. This patch moves the recovery guidance into the shared exec approval unavailable renderer used by the current host=node exec path, then names two concrete recovery paths:
- open the Control UI with `openclaw dashboard --no-open`
- inspect pending approvals with `openclaw approvals get --node <id|name|ip>` or the resolved node id when available

## Tests and validation

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/exec-approval-reply.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-shared.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.tools.test.ts
git diff --check
```

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed:
- the node exec approval unavailable message should include actionable next steps instead of dead-end generic guidance

Environment tested:
- local source checkout on this branch

Evidence after the fix:
- `buildExecApprovalUnavailableReplyPayload({ reason: "no-approval-route", host: "node", nodeId: "mac-1" })` now renders both `openclaw dashboard --no-open` and `openclaw approvals get --node mac-1`
- the host/node metadata is passed from the exec host result and preserved by the deterministic tool-result output path

Observed result:
- users hitting the current shared host=node unavailable path get concrete approval inbox commands in the message itself

What was not tested:
- a live node exec approval timeout/denial flow

## Risk

Low. This is a renderer-only guidance improvement with no behavior change in the approval flow itself.

target.title: fix(memory-core): drop dreaming theme echoes from promotion candidates
target.body:
## Summary

Light Sleep / Deep Sleep phases write
\`\`\`
- Theme: \`X\` kept surfacing across N memories.
\`\`\`
into daily notes. The next dreaming cycle re-ingests the first line as a fresh candidate before the indented \`status: staged\` and \`recalls: 0\` rows land. The 5-field contamination check (\`hasNarrativeLead && hasConfidence && hasEvidence && hasStatus && hasRecalls\`) then misses the snippet, so the theme echo gets promoted into MEMORY.md and the cycle keeps recursing. Real workspaces accumulate dozens of these per agent (verified by inspection of multiple `~/.openclaw/workspace-*/memory/2026-04-*.md` files).

This PR adds a separate guard: when a snippet has a narrative lead AND matches the unique \`kept surfacing across N memories\` signature, mark it as contaminated even without status/recalls. The original 5-field check stays as additional safety.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/short-term-promotion.test.ts` — 45 tests pass, including the new "treats Light Sleep theme echoes as contaminated even without status/recalls" case
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck and tests green for extension lanes
- [x] Existing "does not treat ordinary candidate notes with daily-memory evidence as contaminated" still passes (regular candidates without the theme signature stay through)

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

commands.author: zw-xysk

target.title: fix(tools): treat no-op writes and edits as terminal tool-loop failures (fixes #96983)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96983.

When the `write` tool is called with content identical to the existing file, or the `edit` tool produces unchanged content, the agent loop treats these as progress and retries the same no-op mutation indefinitely.

## Why This Change Was Made

- **write tool**: `readOriginalWriteState` already detects identical content. Now returns a terminal no-op result with `terminate: true` instead of proceeding with the write.
- **edit tool**: Filters out entries where `oldText === newText` before execution. If all entries are no-op, returns a terminal result. If only some are no-op, the valid sibling replacements still execute normally.
- Also catches the `No changes made` error from `edit-diff.ts` and converts it to a terminal result.
- Both tools use `terminate: true` to explicitly signal the tool-loop to stop retrying.

## Changes Made

- `src/agents/sessions/tools/write.ts`: Return `{...textResult(...), terminate: true}` when precheck detects identical content.
- `src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.ts`: Filter out no-op entries (`oldText === newText`) before execution, preserving valid sibling edits. Catch `No changes made` error from edit-diff and return terminal result.
- `src/agents/sessions/tools/write.test.ts`: Added 2 regression tests.
- `src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts`: Added 3 regression tests (all no-op, mixed batch, real changes).

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/agents/sessions/tools/write.test.ts src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts` — 14/14 passed

## Real behavior proof

**Environment:** Linux, OpenClaw 2026.6.10, Node 24.13.1

```
WRITE IDENTICAL: No changes made to demo.txt. The file already has identical content. | terminate: true
WRITE DIFFERENT: Successfully wrote 12 bytes to demo.txt
EDIT NO-OP: No changes made to ... The replacement text is identical to the original. | terminate: true
EDIT MIXED BATCH: Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in ... (valid sibling preserved)
EDIT REAL CHANGE: Successfully replaced 1 block(s) in ...
```

- ✅ Write to existing file with identical content → no-op, `terminate: true`, file unchanged
- ✅ Write with different content → normal success
- ✅ Edit with all no-op entries → `terminate: true`, file unchanged
- ✅ Edit with mixed batch (no-op + real) → valid replacements still execute
- ✅ Edit with real changes → normal replace success

**What was not tested:** E2E agent loop with LLM calling write/edit (requires real model inference).

- [x] AI-assisted

target.title: feat(workboard): add move command to CLI and workboard_move tool
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Workboard agents and operators can create, claim, and complete cards, but there was no direct way to move a card from one column (status) to another. Agents had to rely on the dispatch command or manual store mutations to transition cards, which is awkward for deliberate column changes like "moving a card to Review" or "sending it back to Triage."

Why This Change Was Made

The store already had a move(id, status, position) method, but it was never exposed to the CLI or the agent tools layer. This change wires it up in both directions:

• CLI: workboard move <id> --status <status> for operators and agents running in the terminal
• Tool: workboard_move(id, status, token?) for agent tool use, with claim-scoped access control (only agents that have claimed the card can move it, matching the pattern used by reassign and release)

Status values are validated against the WORKBOARD_STATUSES enum at the tool layer before reaching the store, so invalid statuses are rejected early with a clear error message.

User Impact

• Agents can now move cards between columns as part of their workflow (e.g., moving a card to review after completing implementation)
• Operators can manually move cards via the CLI without writing store queries
• Both CLI and tool outputs support --json for machine-readable consumption

I want to underline that the code was written with AI-assistance. But I went over the code and checked it manually

## Evidence

The following evidence comes from manually conducting terminal runs.  I am limiting the outputs to the relevant lines.

First I create a new card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard create card-abc123 --status tod
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

Here is a list to show the status on the board before moving a card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

OK, now the moving comes:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status running
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

List again to see:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Show details:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard show acc31be5
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving again:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status review
acc31be5  review    normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving back. With JSON:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status triage --json
{
  "card": {
    "id": "acc31be5-2e01-4dd0-93a9-5999779d9a30",
    "title": "card-abc123",
    "status": "triage",
    "priority": "normal",
    "labels": [],
    "position": 1000,
    "createdAt": 1782332679684,
    "updatedAt": 1782333474509,
    "startedAt": 1782333120149,
    "events": [
      {
        "id": "efb13f78-423f-4467-a443-6d61881f8433",
        "kind": "created",
        "at": 1782332679684,
        "toStatus": "todo"
      },
      {
        "id": "df656eb0-3c22-4998-b3db-934e3931836b",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333120149,
        "fromStatus": "todo",
        "toStatus": "running"
      },
      {
        "id": "e9446e73-521a-41b8-bffe-16c8a9efde59",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333379120,
        "fromStatus": "running",
        "toStatus": "review"
      },
      {
        "id": "1f72ee1f-3736-4269-9c95-b6411a08a7db",
        "at": 1782333474509,
        "kind": "moved",
        "fromStatus": "review
        "toStatus": "triage"
      }
    ]
  }
}

What happens if the status is not valid?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status nonexistant
Error: status must be one of: triage, backlog, todo, scheduled, ready, running, review, blocked, done.
Exactly, we want a reasoned error if the column does not exist.

What if the --status flag is missing?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5
Error: --status is required. Use: workboard move <id> --status <status>
This is also correct: a status is necessary to move the card to a target column.

Tests were also conducted:
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts
 RUN  v4.1.8 /home/clawdbot/openclaw
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/tools.test.ts (7 tests) 350ms
 ✓  extensions  extensions/workboard/src/cli.test.ts (10 tests) 55ms
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  17 passed (17)


There is no linked issue for this PR.

@clawsweeper please have a look at it.

target.title: [codex] Update Claude CLI billing docs
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw's Anthropic provider docs still described the previously announced June 15, 2026 Claude Agent SDK / `claude -p` billing change as active. Anthropic's current support article now says that change is paused, so the old wording could mislead users about whether `claude -p` draws from a separate Agent SDK credit or from their subscription usage limits.

## Why This Change Was Made

This updates the Claude CLI billing guidance to match Anthropic's current public support language:

- Anthropic's Agent SDK plan article says the June 15 change is paused and that Claude Agent SDK, `claude -p`, and third-party app usage still draw from subscription usage limits for now: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/15036540-use-the-claude-agent-sdk-with-your-claude-plan
- Anthropic's Pro/Max Claude Code article documents subscription-backed Claude Code usage and plan-limit behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11145838-use-claude-code-with-your-pro-or-max-plan
- Anthropic's Team/Enterprise Claude Code article documents Team and Enterprise subscription behavior: https://support.claude.com/en/articles/11845131-use-claude-code-with-your-team-or-enterprise-plan
- Claude Code's CLI reference still documents `claude -p` as SDK-style non-interactive usage: https://code.claude.com/docs/en/cli-reference

The docs now point users back to Anthropic's support articles before they rely on subscription billing behavior.

## User Impact

Users evaluating Anthropic API keys, Claude CLI reuse, or Claude subscription proxy workflows get current billing guidance and direct official references. The recommendation to use API keys for predictable production or shared automation remains unchanged.

## Evidence

- `corepack pnpm docs:list`
- `corepack pnpm format:docs:check`
- `corepack pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `git diff --check`
- `rg -n "Starting June 15|monthly Agent SDK credit first|no longer draws from normal Claude|separate monthly Agent SDK credit" docs -S` returned no matches

target.title: fix(discord,telegram,github-copilot,google-meet): bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Replace unbounded `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` (1 MiB cap)
across  bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM:4 extensions, preventing OOM from oversized upstream HTTP responses.

## Evidence

### Real HTTP bounded-read proof

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_bounded_json.mts

[proof] real server on :58033, cap=1048576 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes
[proof] small server on :58034

PASS  oversized body: throws bounded cap error :: threw=true msg="Error: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes"
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: server sent ≈ cap bytes, not ~64 MiB :: bytesSent=1048576 (cap=1048576, would-stream=67108864)
PASS  happy path: small JSON parsed correctly :: status=ok id=batch-1
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap :: buffered=33554457 bytes (> 1048576)

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

The proof harness starts a real `node:http` server with no Content-Length, streams ~64 MiB of JSON, and verifies `readResponseWithLimit` cancels the stream at the 1 MiB cap.

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(feishu,browser,msteams,azure-speech,bedrock-mantle,googlechat,huggingface,perplexity): bound JSON response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Replace unbounded `await response.json()` with `readResponseWithLimit` (1 MiB cap)
across  bound JSON response reads to prevent OOM:8 extensions, preventing OOM from oversized upstream HTTP responses.

## Evidence

### Real HTTP bounded-read proof

```
node --import tsx test/_proof_bounded_json.mts

[proof] real server on :58033, cap=1048576 bytes, would-stream≈67108864 bytes
[proof] small server on :58034

PASS  oversized body: throws bounded cap error :: threw=true msg="Error: JSON response exceeds 1048576 bytes"
PASS  stream cancelled at cap: server sent ≈ cap bytes, not ~64 MiB :: bytesSent=1048576 (cap=1048576, would-stream=67108864)
PASS  happy path: small JSON parsed correctly :: status=ok id=batch-1
PASS  negative control: unbounded read buffers PAST cap :: buffered=33554457 bytes (> 1048576)

[proof] 4 PASS, 0 FAIL
```

The proof harness starts a real `node:http` server with no Content-Length, streams ~64 MiB of JSON, and verifies `readResponseWithLimit` cancels the stream at the 1 MiB cap.

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(edit): unwrap XML-RPC {item: {oldText, newText}} edit entries
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Unwrap `{item: {oldText, newText}}` entries from XML-RPC array-of-structs
transport (reported with DeepSeek/XML-RPC paths). The wire format wraps each
array element as `[{item: {oldText, newText}}]` instead of the canonical
`[{oldText, newText}]`.

## Changes

- `src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.ts`: Add XML-RPC unwrap logic in both
  `prepareEditArguments` and `execute()` paths

## Evidence

### XML-RPC {item: ...} entry → canonical {oldText, newText}
```
Input:  [{item: {oldText: "original content", newText: "replaced content"}}]
Output: [{oldText: "original content", newText: "replaced content"}]
```

### Mixed entries (item-wrapped + canonical) both work
```
Input:  [{oldText: "line one", newText: "LINE ONE"}, {item: {oldText: "line three", newText: "LINE THREE"}}]
Output: Both edits applied correctly
```

_AI-assisted._

commands.author: lsr911

target.title: fix(opencode-go): treat block-boundary SSE events as liveness for idle timer
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Treat block-boundary SSE events (`text_end`, `thinking_end`,
`toolcall_start`, `toolcall_end`) as liveness signals that reset the
idle timer, preventing premature stream abort when the model pauses at
block boundaries.

## Changes

- `extensions/opencode-go/stream-termination.ts`: Add 4 block-boundary
  event types to `isProviderProgressEvent` so the idle timeout is reset
  at block transitions.

## Evidence

Before: Block-boundary SSE events did not reset the idle timer, causing
premature abort on slow streams with quiet block transitions.

After: The idle timer is reset at `text_end`, `thinking_end`,
`toolcall_start`, and `toolcall_end` events, so the stream survives
normal block-boundary pauses.

_AI-assisted._

commands.author: lsr911

target.title: fix(memory): backfill provider.model in createWithAdapter when adapter returns empty string
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Backfill `provider.model` when the adapter returns an empty string so callers
always see the resolved model name. An empty model string during boot can
overwrite a correct index identity and leave the search index permanently dirty.

## Changes

- Call `resolveProviderModel` once and reuse the resolved model
- Backfill `result.provider.model` with `resolvedModel` when adapter returns
  an empty string: `result.provider ? { ...result.provider, model: result.provider.model || resolvedModel } : null`

## Evidence

### Before (empty model → dirty index)
```
provider.model = ""  // adapter returned empty string, index identity was lost
```

### After (model backfilled from resolved default)
```
provider.model = "text-embedding-3-small"  // resolved from adapter.defaultModel
```

_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(agent): surface non_deliverable_terminal_turn with a specific retry message
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Surfaces `non_deliverable_terminal_turn` with a specific retry message instead
of the generic "Agent couldn't generate a response" fallback. The marker was
already classified but never surfaced to users.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/types.ts` | Add `terminalError` to `EmbeddedRunAttemptResult` type |
| `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/types.ts` | Add `terminalError` to `EmbeddedAgentRunMeta` type |
| `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts` | Propagate `terminalError` from attemptTrajectoryTerminal |
| `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run.ts` | Spread `terminalError` into incomplete_turn meta blocks |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-lifecycle-terminal.ts` | Add `terminalError` to deferred terminal metadata keys |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-failure-copy.ts` | Add `resolveExternalRunFailureTextForTerminalError` + constants |
| `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` | Wire `terminalError` into `buildExternalRunFailureReply` |

## Evidence

### Unit tests: terminal error propagation + fallback copy routing

```
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt-trajectory-status.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  233 passed (233)
   Duration  98.06s
```

### Retry-first copy

When the model stops between tool calls without producing a reply:
> ⚠️ The model stopped between tool calls without producing a reply. Please
> try the same message again — this usually resolves on retry. If it
> persists, use /new or a lighter model.

instead of the old generic fallback.

_AI-assisted._

commands.author: ZOOWH

target.title: fix(lmstudio): pass configured contextWindow to embedding model preload
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

LM Studio embedding preload calls `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` without `requestedContextLength`, causing LM Studio to load models with the maximum supported context length (32K). On GPUs with limited VRAM this triggers CUDA OOM.

**Root cause**: The embedding provider path in `createLmstudioEmbeddingProvider` omitted `requestedContextLength` from the `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded` call, unlike the inference path which resolves it via `resolveRequestedContextLength`.

**Fix**: Resolve `requestedContextLength` from the model config (`contextTokens` → `contextWindow`) with provider-level fallback, and pass it to `ensureLmstudioModelLoaded`.

Closes #97016

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope

- [x] Extension (LM Studio provider)

## Evidence

**Before fix (main @ 80c754d):** The embedding provider call omits `requestedContextLength`.

**After fix:** Model-level contextWindow=8192 → passed as `requestedContextLength`. Provider-level fallback when model has no config. Omits when neither has config.

**Test evidence:** 5/5 new regression tests pass (model contextWindow, model contextTokens priority, provider fallback, provider contextTokens priority, no-config omit). Existing LM Studio tests: 98/98 pass.

**Runtime proof:**
```text
$ node --import tsx --no-warnings proof-97016.mjs
model contextWindow=8192 → 8192
model contextTokens=4096 (over contextWindow=32768) → 4096
provider contextWindow=16384 → 16384
provider contextTokens=2048 → 2048
no config → undefined
✅ All assertions passed
```

**Not tested:** Live LM Studio with CUDA GPU. Verified via real production SDK function (`asPositiveSafeInteger`) and resolution precedence logic.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

commands.author: maweibin

target.title: fix(edit): show candidate lines with similarity score on oldText match failure
target.body:
Fixes #97032.

## What Problem This Solves

When the edit tool's `oldText` match fails, it only returns "Could not find the exact text" with no diagnostic information. Users don't know whether the issue is wrong escaping, incorrect indentation, or whitespace mismatch — they can only guess and retry.

## Why This Change Was Made

Extended `getNotFoundError()` to compute Levenshtein-based similarity scores between the oldText and file content lines. On match failure, the error now includes up to 3 nearest candidate lines with line numbers, similarity percentages, and a hint to check whitespace/indentation.

Inputs are capped (200 lines, 200 chars per line) to prevent unbounded CPU on large files. The matching algorithm itself is unchanged.

## User Impact

Edit tool users now see actionable diagnostics on match failure:
```
Could not find the exact text in test.js.
  near line 2: "  console.log(\"Hello\");" (90% match, check whitespace/indentation)
  near line 1: "function hello() {" (30% match, check whitespace/indentation)
```

## Evidence

**Behavior addressed**: Edit tool oldText match failure lacks candidate diagnostics.

**Real environment tested**: Node 24.13.1, direct `applyEditsToNormalizedContent` call.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node --import tsx -e "
import { applyEditsToNormalizedContent } from './src/agents/sessions/tools/edit-diff.js';
try {
  applyEditsToNormalizedContent(
    'function hello() {\n  console.log(\"Hello\");\n  return 42;\n}\n',
    [{ oldText: 'console.log(\"hello\")', newText: 'console.log(\"Hi\")' }],
    'test.js',
  );
} catch(e) { console.log(e.message); }
"
```

**Evidence after fix**:
```
$ node --import tsx -e "..."
Could not find the exact text in test.js. The old text must match exactly including all whitespace and newlines.
  near line 2: "  console.log(\"Hello\");" (90% match, check whitespace/indentation)
  near line 1: "function hello() {" (30% match, check whitespace/indentation)
```

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts --run
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
  Tests      75 passed (75)
```

**Observed result after fix**: On oldText match failure, up to 3 nearest candidate lines are displayed with similarity scores. Whitespace/indentation differences are visible in the candidate output. Large files are bounded at 200 lines / 200 chars per line.


## Regression Test Plan

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit.test.ts src/agents/agent-tools.create-openclaw-coding-tools.test.ts --run`
  - Test Files  2 passed (2)
  - Tests      75 passed (75)
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/agents/sessions/tools/edit-diff.ts` — clean

## AI Assistance

- **AI-assisted**: Yes
- **Model used**: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context)
- **Co-Authored-By**: Already in commit message

target.title: fix: support baseURL alias in memory embedding provider config resolution
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

When users configure `models.providers.openai.baseURL` (camelCase with capital R) pointing to a local Ollama or OpenAI-compatible endpoint, `ModelProviderSchema` silently drops the `baseURL` key via `.strict()`. Memory embeddings then fall through to the built-in OpenAI adapter and route to `api.openai.com/v1` instead of the configured local endpoint.

Fixes #93396

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/config/zod-schema.core.ts:495-522` | Accept `baseURL` as optional field + normalize to `baseUrl` in `superRefine`. Downstream code only reads `baseUrl` — no changes needed. URL redaction is auto-fixed (covers `baseUrl` paths). |
| `src/agents/memory-search.ts:182-185` | Skip built-in adapter when provider has custom `baseUrl`. Before: a `providerId === "openai"` match returned the direct adapter unconditionally. After: checks `providerConfig.baseUrl` and skips when configured. |
| `src/config/schema.test.ts` | +2 tests: normalization of `baseURL` → `baseUrl`, and `baseUrl` priority over `baseURL` |
| `src/agents/memory-search.test.ts` | +2 tests: skip adapter with custom baseUrl, use adapter without one |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Fix for issue #93396 — `models.providers.*.baseURL` is accepted by `.strict()` and normalized to canonical `baseUrl`, routing memory embeddings to the configured endpoint instead of `api.openai.com/v1`.

**Real setup tested**: Node.js v24.13.1 on Linux x86_64

**Exact steps or command run after fix**:
```bash
# 1. Schema normalization — real production code path
node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

# 2. Tests
cd $WORKTREE
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ node --import tsx /tmp/verify-baseurl-normalize.mjs

=== Test 1: baseURL → baseUrl normalization ===
Input:  { baseURL: 'http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1' }
Output: baseUrl = "http://127.0.0.1:11434/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 2: baseUrl already set → baseURL ignored ===
Input:  baseUrl='https://my-proxy.example.com/v1', baseURL='http://ignore-me.local:11434/v1'
Output: baseUrl = "https://my-proxy.example.com/v1"
PASS: true

=== Test 3: Memory search routing ===
Provider 'openai' with custom baseURL:
  embedding provider: skipped (custom endpoint)
PASS: true (built-in adapter skipped)

✅ All 3 real-behavior checks pass

$ pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
 ✓ |unit-fast| src/agents/memory-search.test.ts (37 tests)
 ✓ |runtime-config| src/config/schema.test.ts (57 tests)

 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `baseURL` passes `.strict()` validation. The existing `superRefine` normalizes it to `baseUrl` in-place, so all downstream call sites (memory search, provider config, embedding client, remote client, gateway `/v1/embeddings`) work without changes — they already read `baseUrl`. URL redaction also works because `baseUrl` is already in the sensitive URL path list.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end with a running Ollama instance serving embeddings. Schema normalization and routing logic are independently verified.

## Tests and validation

```bash
pnpm test src/agents/memory-search.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts
# Test Files  2 passed (2)
#      Tests  94 passed (94)
```

*AI-assisted: built with Claude Code*

target.title: fix(mcp): strip null optional arguments before MCP tool calls
target.body:
Fixes #96716

## What Problem This Solves

MCP tool calls with optional arguments set to JSON null fail via OpenClaw but succeed via other MCP clients. The MCP server distinguishes null from absent/undefined, so sending `{"insight_id": null}` triggers a different code path than omitting `insight_id` entirely.

## Evidence

### Fix

`src/agents/agent-bundle-mcp-materialize.ts`: strip null values from tool call input before passing to `callTool()`.

### Terminal output

```
========================================
  PR #96718 — MCP Null Argument Strip
========================================
  Original: {"category":null,"cluster_name":"testenv","insight_id":null}
  Cleaned:  {"cluster_name":"testenv"}
  insight_id present: false
  category present:   false
  Status: PASS
========================================
```

### What was not tested

End-to-end with a real MCP server. The fix is a targeted null-strip at the boundary between OpenClaw's tool call dispatch and the MCP runtime.

## Changes

1 file, +9/-1. Lockfile unchanged.

target.title: fix(agents): emit user-visible notice when a turn exhausts its time budget
target.body:
## Update — rebased onto current `main` (2026-06-25)

The branch had drifted ~5,800 commits behind `main` (ClawSweeper flagged it conflicting). It is now rebased; the 3-commit series applies on current `main`:

- `run.ts` and `run/incomplete-turn.ts` resolved cleanly — the `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice` signature is reconciled to the current `allowSilentReply` form, and the integration tests were re-seated inside the runner describe block.
- **Re-verified on current `main` (Node 22):** `run.incomplete-turn.test.ts` **244/244 passing** (the 3 turn-budget integration tests through the real `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path + the 8-case `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice` decision matrix); **`tsgo:core` clean**; **`tsgo:test:src` clean**.

The committed fault-injection regression tests drive the **real** `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path (not a mock of it). A live gateway/channel recording of a ≥`timeoutSeconds` tool-execution timeout remains the one artifact reproducible only on a long-running real agent.

---

## Problem

When an agent turn exhausts its time budget (`agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds`) or a run-level deadline **while no assistant reply has been produced**, the gateway can go completely silent — no message reaches the channel, no indication a limit was hit. From a user's seat this is indistinguishable from a hung process.

This is not the prompt/provider timeout case, which already surfaces *"Request timed out before a response was generated…"*. It's the timeout that fires **outside a model prompt** — most commonly while a tool is executing on a complex turn:

- `attempt.timedOut` is `true`, but because `countActiveToolExecutions(...) > 0` the runner also sets `timedOutDuringToolExecution = true`;
- `timedOutDuringPrompt = timedOut && !timedOutDuringCompaction && !timedOutDuringToolExecution` is therefore `false`, so the existing prompt-timeout notice is skipped;
- with no partial assistant text there's no `incompleteTurnText`, so the run falls through to the terminal "embedded run done" path, returns `payloads: undefined`, and the reply layer returns silently on the empty payload array.

## Fix

Add one terminal branch in `runEmbeddedAgent` that surfaces a recoverable, user-visible notice when a turn timed out and is about to deliver nothing visible — mirroring the prompt-level timeout copy already emitted from the same function. Because the branch returns a non-empty payload, it flows through the normal gateway-agnostic reply path (Discord, Matrix, etc.) and never reaches the silent return.

The decision is a small pure helper, `resolveTurnBudgetTimeoutNotice(...)`, returning the notice only when correct and `null` otherwise. It keys on **`timedOut`** (distinct from a user cancel) and is suppressed for the turns that are silent/handled by design:

- `timedOutDuringCompaction` — compaction timeouts are **paused/resumable**, not abandoned; emitting a notice (passed as `incompleteTurnText`) would also override the `paused` liveness state the terminal path preserves;
- `allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` (the **raw** flag — note the derived `shouldTreatEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent` is always `false` for timed-out attempts, so it cannot protect this case) — cron/heartbeat/autonomous contexts;
- a messaging tool already delivered out-of-band (`hasMessagingToolDeliveryEvidence`);
- a deterministic approval prompt was delivered out-of-band (`didSendDeterministicApprovalPrompt`; payload building intentionally suppresses assistant artifacts there).

A separate copy variant covers an idle (no-output) model timeout. Net change is additive: it only produces output where there is currently none.

## Proof

**1. Committed real-`runEmbeddedAgent` regression tests** (`run.incomplete-turn.test.ts`) drive the real terminal path via the existing harness loader:

- tool-execution timeout, empty reply → notice delivered, `livenessState: "abandoned"`;
- same, in a silent-reply context (`allowEmptyAssistantReplyAsSilent: true`) → **no notice** (stays silent);
- compaction timeout → **no notice**, `livenessState: "paused"` preserved.

Plus full unit coverage of the helper's decision matrix. Suite: **123 passing**; `tsgo:core` and oxlint clean.

**2. Before/after through the real terminal path** (fault-injected timed-out attempt, real `runEmbeddedAgent`):

```
# tool-execution timeout, empty reply
BEFORE (origin/main):  result.payloads = undefined                 livenessState = blocked   → silent
AFTER  (this PR):       result.payloads = [{ text: "⚠️ I hit my time budget on this request and
                        stopped before finishing. Ask me to continue, or simplify the request.
                        If this happens often, raise `agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` in your
                        config.", isError: true }]                  livenessState = abandoned → delivered

# compaction timeout, empty reply  (regression guard)
AFTER:                  result.payloads = undefined                 livenessState = paused    → correctly silent
```

**On a live gateway/channel capture:** the trigger is a ≥`agents.defaults.timeoutSeconds` (default 600s) budget exhausted *during tool execution* — not reproducible against a real channel in CI or by an external contributor without a long-running live agent. The fault-injection harness drives the **real** `runEmbeddedAgent` terminal path (`makeAttemptResult({ timedOut: true, timedOutDuringToolExecution: true, assistantTexts: [] })`) and the committed tests assert end-to-end behavior through the same function; the harness itself is kept out of the committed diff to keep the patch narrow.

commands.author: zw-xysk

target.title: fix(tls): add subjectAltName to auto-generated gateway self-signed certificate (fixes #96910)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Fixes #96910.

When gateway TLS is enabled with `autoGenerate=true` (the default), the self-signed certificate is generated with only `CN=openclaw-gateway` and no `subjectAltName` extension. Node.js ≥17 ignores the CN for hostname/IP verification per RFC 6125, so the CLI probe to `wss://127.0.0.1:18789` fails with:

```
Gateway probe failed: Hostname/IP does not match certificate's altnames: IP: 127.0.0.1 is not in the cert's list:
```

## Why This Change Was Made

`openssl req -x509` was invoked without `-addext subjectAltName=...`, producing a CN-only certificate. Modern TLS stacks require SANs for hostname matching, especially IP addresses.

## User Impact

Users with `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true` (default) no longer get TLS handshake failures when the CLI or other local clients connect to the gateway by IP or hostname.

Existing users who already have a CN-only certificate generated by a previous OpenClaw version will have it **automatically detected and regenerated** with SANs on the next gateway start. A warning log is emitted when this happens.

## Changes Made

- `src/infra/tls/gateway.ts`:
  - Added `import os` and `-addext "subjectAltName=..."` to `generateSelfSignedCert`
  - Added `isOpenClawGeneratedCnOnlyCert()` to detect old CN-only certs
  - Added `maybeUpgradeCnOnlyCert()` to regenerate old certs with SANs
  - Added `buildTlsResult()` helper to reduce duplication
- `src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts`: Added regression tests for new SAN generation and CN-only cert upgrade

## Evidence

- **Unit tests:** `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run src/infra/tls/gateway.test.ts` — 9/9 passed

## Real behavior proof

### Proof 1: New certificate contains SAN

```
$ openssl x509 -in gateway-cert.pem -noout -ext subjectAltName
X509v3 Subject Alternative Name:
    DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

```
$ node -e "... tls.checkServerIdentity(127.0.0.1, cert)"
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for 127.0.0.1
✅ STRICT TLS CHECK PASSED for localhost
```

### Proof 2: Existing CN-only certificate is auto-upgraded

**Before:**
```
BEFORE - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
BEFORE - subjectAltName: (none)
```

**During (log):**
```
[warn] gateway tls: upgrading CN-only certificate to include subjectAltName
[info] gateway tls: generated self-signed cert at /tmp/.../cert.pem
```

**After:**
```
AFTER - subject: CN=openclaw-gateway
AFTER - subjectAltName: DNS:localhost, DNS:LIN-66D8BD4EA2C, IP Address:127.0.0.1, IP Address:0:0:0:0:0:0:0:1
```

### Scope

- ✅ Only OpenClaw-generated certs (`CN=openclaw-gateway`, no SAN) are upgraded
- ✅ User-provided certs (`caPath` or non-OpenClaw certs) are **untouched**
- ✅ Upgrade only happens when `autoGenerate=true` (the default)
- ✅ Logged with `warn` level for full traceability

**What was not tested:** Live non-loopback TLS client handshake with external bind addresses (requires security review to determine acceptable SAN scope).

- [x] AI-assisted

target.title: feat(workboard): add move command to CLI and workboard_move tool
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

Workboard agents and operators can create, claim, and complete cards, but there was no direct way to move a card from one column (status) to another. Agents had to rely on the dispatch command or manual store mutations to transition cards, which is awkward for deliberate column changes like "moving a card to Review" or "sending it back to Triage."

Why This Change Was Made

The store already had a move(id, status, position) method, but it was never exposed to the CLI or the agent tools layer. This change wires it up in both directions:

• CLI: workboard move <id> --status <status> for operators and agents running in the terminal
• Tool: workboard_move(id, status, token?) for agent tool use, with claim-scoped access control (only agents that have claimed the card can move it, matching the pattern used by reassign and release)

Status values are validated against the WORKBOARD_STATUSES enum at the tool layer before reaching the store, so invalid statuses are rejected early with a clear error message.

User Impact

• Agents can now move cards between columns as part of their workflow (e.g., moving a card to review after completing implementation)
• Operators can manually move cards via the CLI without writing store queries
• Both CLI and tool outputs support --json for machine-readable consumption

I want to underline that the code was written with AI-assistance. But I went over the code and checked it manually

## Evidence

The following evidence comes from manually conducting terminal runs.  I am limiting the outputs to the relevant lines.

First I create a new card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard create card-abc123 --status tod
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

Here is a list to show the status on the board before moving a card
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  todo      normal  default  card-abc123

OK, now the moving comes:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status running
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

List again to see:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard list
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Show details:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard show acc31be5
acc31be5  running   normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving again:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status review
acc31be5  review    normal  default  card-abc123

Try moving back. With JSON:
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status triage --json
{
  "card": {
    "id": "acc31be5-2e01-4dd0-93a9-5999779d9a30",
    "title": "card-abc123",
    "status": "triage",
    "priority": "normal",
    "labels": [],
    "position": 1000,
    "createdAt": 1782332679684,
    "updatedAt": 1782333474509,
    "startedAt": 1782333120149,
    "events": [
      {
        "id": "efb13f78-423f-4467-a443-6d61881f8433",
        "kind": "created",
        "at": 1782332679684,
        "toStatus": "todo"
      },
      {
        "id": "df656eb0-3c22-4998-b3db-934e3931836b",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333120149,
        "fromStatus": "todo",
        "toStatus": "running"
      },
      {
        "id": "e9446e73-521a-41b8-bffe-16c8a9efde59",
        "kind": "moved",
        "at": 1782333379120,
        "fromStatus": "running",
        "toStatus": "review"
      },
      {
        "id": "1f72ee1f-3736-4269-9c95-b6411a08a7db",
        "at": 1782333474509,
        "kind": "moved",
        "fromStatus": "review
        "toStatus": "triage"
      }
    ]
  }
}

What happens if the status is not valid?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5 --status nonexistant
Error: status must be one of: triage, backlog, todo, scheduled, ready, running, review, blocked, done.
Exactly, we want a reasoned error if the column does not exist.

What if the --status flag is missing?
$ pnpm openclaw --dev workboard move acc31be5
Error: --status is required. Use: workboard move <id> --status <status>
This is also correct: a status is necessary to move the card to a target column.

Tests were also conducted:

commands.author: jleonelion

target.title: feat(codex): scope user MCP server attachment by the tool allowlist
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

An OpenClaw operator running a **Codex-runtime agent** cannot give that agent access to a configured MCP server without also granting it Codex's full native code-mode shell/filesystem surface. The two are conflated behind a single gate.

In `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.ts`, `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` returns true only when the effective tool allowlist contains a literal `*`. That one boolean (`nativeToolSurfaceEnabled`) is then reused, unchanged, in `attempt-startup.ts` as `userMcpServersEnabled` — which decides whether `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projects `cfg.mcp.servers` into the Codex thread at all. Consequences:

1. **All-or-nothing.** Any least-privilege allowlist (`tools.allow` / `tools.alsoAllow` without `*`) silently disables **every** user MCP server, even ones the allowlist explicitly names. The MCP process spawns but its tools never register in the compiled toolset.
2. **Over-exposure when servers do attach.** `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` projected **every** enabled `cfg.mcp.servers` entry, filtered only by the per-server `codex.agents` field — it never consulted the tool allowlist.

This is a general gap for any scoped Codex agent with any MCP server, not a single integration.

## What Changed

Decouple the two capabilities and make MCP attachment flow through the **same allowlist contract OpenClaw already uses for every other tool** (`tool-policy.ts`):

- **Native code mode is unchanged** — `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` keeps its fail-closed, wildcard-only rule.
- **`shouldEnableUserMcpServersForCodexAppServer`** now answers only *"is it safe to attach external MCP servers this turn?"* (not a memory-flush run; OpenClaw sandbox, if active, can be honored). The per-server decision moves to the projection.
- **`resolveCodexMcpServerAllow`** matches against the **provider-safe model-facing tool ids** OpenClaw exposes — `<sanitizedServer>__<sanitizedTool>` via `sanitizeServerName` + `normalizeToolName`, **not** the raw `mcp.servers` key — and honors the same entries as the bundle-MCP allowlist contract:
  - `undefined` → include, all tools (legacy unrestricted behavior preserved)
  - `*`, `bundle-mcp`, `group:plugins` → include, all tools
  - `<server>__*` → include that server, all tools
  - `<server>__<tool>` → include that server, scoped to the named tool(s)
  - no matching token → exclude the server
- **`buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch`** projects **only** referenced servers, and for exact-tool entries emits Codex's per-server **`enabled_tools`** filter so other tools from an attached server stay unreachable. The thread-config fingerprint varies with the patch, so a thread rebinds when scope changes.

### Addresses ClawSweeper review
- **Provider-safe matching** (was raw-key): `Outlook Graph` (ids `outlook-graph__*`) resolves; `bundle-mcp` / `group:plugins` honored.
- **Per-tool scope** (was discarded): exact entries emit `enabled_tools` instead of attaching the whole server. (`enabled_tools` carries the sanitized tool fragment; exact for provider-safe names, fail-closed otherwise, since the live tool list isn't known at projection time.)

## Files
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/{dynamic-tool-build,run-attempt,attempt-startup,thread-lifecycle}.ts` — decouple + plumb `userMcpServersEnabled`, pass `toolsAllow` to the projection
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.ts` — `resolveCodexMcpServerAllow` + allowlist-aware projection with `enabled_tools`
- `docs/plugins/codex-harness.md`; tests as below

## Evidence

### Unit tests (`vitest run`)
- `src/agents/cli-runner/bundle-mcp-codex.user-config.test.ts` — **24 passed** (undefined/`*`/`bundle-mcp`/`group:plugins` attach all; `opik__*` only opik, no `enabled_tools`; exact `opik__list,opik__read` → `enabled_tools:["list","read"]`; provider-safe `Outlook Graph` ↔ `outlook-graph__*`; `["message"]`/`[]` → none).
- `extensions/codex/src/app-server/dynamic-tool-build.test.ts` — **51 passed**, incl. the assertion that `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface(["opik__*"]) === false` — **native code mode stays disabled** under a scoped MCP allowlist.

### Runtime proof — patched OpenClaw projection (real code, unmocked)
Driving the actual `buildCodexUserMcpServersThreadConfigPatch` over a config with `opik` + `notion` + `atlas`:
```
toolsAllow=undefined                    -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["*"]                        -> servers=["opik","notion","atlas"]  scope=all tools each
toolsAllow=["opik__*"]                  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["opik__list","opik__read"]  -> servers=["opik"]                    scope={"opik":["list","read"]}
toolsAllow=["notion__*"]                -> servers=["notion"]                  scope={"notion":"(all tools)"}
toolsAllow=["message","web_search"]     -> servers=[]                          scope=none
```

### Runtime proof — real Codex app-server (`codex-cli 0.139.0`)
Drove the **real** `codex app-server` over JSON-RPC stdio with two stub stdio MCP servers (each exposing `list,read,write,schema`): `opik` configured with `enabled_tools=["list"]` (what the projection emits for `["opik__list"]`), `notion` unfiltered (the `["notion__*"]` case). Then `mcpServerStatus/list` (`detail: toolsAndAuthOnly`):
```
=== LIVE MCP TOOLS REGISTERED BY CODEX ===
opik:   [list]                       <- enabled_tools filter honored by the real binary
notion: [list, read, schema, write]  <- unscoped server, all tools
(other entries omitted: unrelated servers from local ~/.codex/config.toml)
```
This confirms the real Codex binary (a) treats `enabled_tools`/`disabled_tools` as first-class per-server fields (visible in `codex mcp get` schema output) and (b) registers **only** the scoped tool when the filter is set.

### Types
Full-CI `check-prod-types` / `check-lint` / `check-docs` pass; standalone extension `tsc` shows only pre-existing plugin-sdk `dist`-sync noise, none referencing this PR's symbols.

## Security posture
- Native code-mode shell/file access still requires a literal `*` — unchanged (proven above).
- Default-open for unrestricted (`undefined`) agents preserved; sandbox-without-exec-server still attaches no user MCP servers.
- Net effect is *tighter*: scoped allowlists attach only referenced servers; exact-tool entries attach only named tools via `enabled_tools`.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(openrouter): bound media response reads
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Success-path untrusted OpenRouter image/video response bodies used unbounded reads, which could OOM or hang the process if a provider streamed a large JSON body without Content-Length.

## Changes
- Replace the OpenRouter image success-path `response.json()` read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse` bounded parsing.
- Replace the OpenRouter video submit/poll success-path JSON read with shared `readProviderJsonResponse`, while preserving the existing malformed-response error for invalid JSON.
- Let OpenRouter video use the existing provider request config for private-network allowance instead of forcing the resolved request config to `false`; the default remains denied through the shared config helper.
- Add focused OpenRouter image/video tests for the bounded helper path and oversized submit-body cancellation.

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed
- OpenRouter image and video success-path JSON responses now stop at the shared 16MiB cap and abort the streaming TCP response when exceeded.

Real environment tested
- Local Node.js v22.22.0 process using `node:http` loopback TCP servers, no `Content-Length`, true `fetch`, and the real exported OpenRouter image/video provider builders.

Exact steps
- `node --import tsx scratchpad/openrouter-bound-proof.mjs`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo`
- `.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main`

Observed result
- Bounded OpenRouter image/video success JSON throws `exceeds 16777216 bytes`, aborts the server socket, and sends fewer bytes than the full oversized body.
- Raw `response.json()` negative control buffers the full oversized body.
- Small OpenRouter image/video JSON responses parse normally.

What was not tested
- Live OpenRouter API calls.
- The optional OpenRouter generation-cost lookup was not changed in this PR.

## Evidence
```text
$ node --import tsx scratchpad/openrouter-bound-proof.mjs
PASS openrouter image success JSON: threw "OpenRouter image generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"; socket abort=true; bytesSent=16842764; full=17825792
PASS openrouter video submit success JSON: threw "OpenRouter video generation: JSON response exceeds 16777216 bytes"; socket abort=true; bytesSent=16842764; full=17825792
PASS negative control: raw response.json() buffered 17825792 bytes; bytesSent=17825806; cap=16777216
PASS happy path: OpenRouter image small JSON parsed normally
PASS happy path: OpenRouter video small JSON parsed normally
PASS openrouter node:http bounded success-path proof complete

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/openrouter/image-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/video-generation-provider.test.ts extensions/openrouter/index.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  67 passed (67)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 128.39s

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions.tsbuildinfo
(exit code 0)

$ node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test.tsbuildinfo
(exit code 0)

$ .agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode branch --base upstream/main
autoreview clean: no accepted/actionable findings reported
overall: patch is correct (0.86)
```

**Label**: security
_AI-assisted._

target.title: fix(inworld): bound TTS audio and error response body reads to prevent OOM
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

This exists to stop Inworld's externally controlled streaming TTS and error responses from buffering attacker-sized bodies into memory. The Inworld streaming TTS endpoint read its response with an unbounded `await response.text()` (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:97`) before splitting newline-delimited JSON and concatenating base64 audio chunks, so a controlled or hijacked endpoint could stream an arbitrary body and force OpenClaw to buffer it. The non-OK error body (`extensions/inworld/tts.ts:93`) and the analogous `listInworldVoices` error path had the same unbounded-read shape.

## Changes

- Bound the main `inworldTTS` audio response with `readResponseWithLimit`, using `INWORLD_TTS_BODY_MAX_BYTES = MAX_AUDIO_BYTES * 2` (32 MiB) plus a 30s idle timeout.
- Bound Inworld error-body reads with `readResponseTextSnippet` so hostile error responses cannot inline unbounded bodies into thrown messages.
- Re-exported `readResponseTextSnippet` from `openclaw/plugin-sdk/response-limit-runtime` so extensions use the existing response-limit entry instead of a new helper path.
- Added Inworld TTS regression coverage for oversized audio streams, bounded error snippets, cancellation, and normal payload decoding.

## Evidence

The earlier proof for this PR used an in-process stream fixture, so this proof was upgraded to a real terminal `node:http` check. The scratchpad proof script is local only and is not committed. It starts a real loopback CONNECT proxy because Inworld's production SSRF policy allowlists the configured hostname and does not permit a direct loopback base URL; the exported `inworldTTS` still drives the real `fetchWithSsrFGuard` / undici dispatcher path, `readResponseWithLimit`, `readResponseTextSnippet`, and a real TCP socket. No in-process `ReadableStream` fixture is used.

Command: `node --import tsx scratchpad/inworld-http-proof.mts`

```text
[proof] real node:http loopback proxy on http://127.0.0.1:33889, target=http://example.com, audioCap=33554432 bytes, audioWouldStream=134217728 bytes
PASS  oversized Inworld TTS audio stream throws bounded error :: threw=true msg="Inworld TTS audio stream too large: 33619968 bytes (limit: 33554432 bytes)"
PASS  audio stream cancelled: proxy saw socket abort before full body :: aborted=true bytesSent=35651584 (<134217728) chunks=34
PASS  audio stream cancelled near the 32 MiB cap :: bytesSent=35651584 (<67108864)
PASS  negative control: raw unbounded read buffers the full body past the 32 MiB cap :: buffered=134217728 bytes (> 33554432)
PASS  negative control consumed far more than bounded read :: unbounded buffered=134217728 vs bounded sent≈35651584
PASS  oversized error body stays bounded and cancels stream :: messageLength=430 aborted=true bytesSent=1048576 (<33554432) cap=8192
PASS  happy path: small NDJSON audio payload still parses :: audio="real-audio-bytes" bytes=16

[proof] 8 PASS, 0 FAIL
[proof] ALL PASS
```

Focused regression test:

Command: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/inworld/tts.test.ts`

```text
Test Files  1 passed (1)
Tests       19 passed (19)
Result      [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 24.04s
```

## Symmetry with #95108

#95108 bounded the analogous Anthropic Messages error-response read with `readResponseWithLimit` / `readResponseTextSnippet`. This PR is the symmetric fix for the Inworld TTS main audio stream plus its error bodies, reusing the same `@openclaw/media-core` helper through the `response-limit-runtime` plugin entry rather than introducing a new abstraction.

**Label**: security

---

AI-assisted: implemented and verified with AI assistance; the proof commands above were run for real against the actual code path.
